ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific node for 2-in-1 instant white coffee with creamer and no sugar. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers instant coffee powder; the creamer component is not separately coded. Closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept found for 2-in-1 instant white coffee with creamer. Null assigned to avoid fabrication.

allergens: milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain casein)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 ml (200 g) prepared from one sachet dissolved in hot water, as commonly consumed in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: 2-in-1 White Coffee (No Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 2-in-1 white coffee (no sugar), cr_id D09010005. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat composition typical of palm-based non-dairy creamer (approximately 65% of total fat as saturated fat), as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this estimate is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — coffee and coffee-based beverages with creamer
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 2-in-1 instant white coffee with creamer and no sugar. GI estimated at 41 (low) based on analogous instant coffee with non-dairy creamer entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The low GI reflects the minimal sugar content (1.43 g/100g) and the fat-slowing effect of the creamer on gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (41 × 7.98 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 6.5; reported as 5.9 using available carbohydrate fraction. Estimation basis documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (27 mg/100 g) and no added sugar; caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: With only 27 mg sodium per 100 g and no added sugar, this beverage does not trigger sodium or SSB thresholds for hypertension; however, caffeine intake should be limited to no more than 2 servings per day to avoid acute blood pressure elevation, and the palm-based creamer should be checked for saturated fat content that may contribute to arterial stiffness over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low sugar (1.43 g/100 g), no added sugar, low GI (~41), moderate carbohydrate (7.98 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low sugar content (1.43 g/100 g), absence of added sugar, and estimated low GI (~41) make this beverage suitable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed without additional sugar; individuals should verify the creamer ingredient list does not contain glucose syrup or maltodextrin, which would raise the effective glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~1.52 g/100 g) from palm-based non-dairy creamer; low sodium and low calorie profile are favourable
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The estimated saturated fat content of approximately 1.52 g/100 g from the palm-based non-dairy creamer warrants moderate caution for cardiovascular disease patients; the low sodium (27 mg/100 g) and low calorie (56 kcal/100 g) profile are favourable, but regular consumption should be balanced against cumulative saturated fat intake from the creamer, and a label check for trans fat is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of positive anti-inflammatory factors; palm-based creamer may contribute saturated fat; coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) are present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) have some anti-inflammatory properties, the absence of omega-3 fatty acids, significant dietary fibre, or other established anti-inflammatory nutrients means this beverage does not meet the positive anti-inflammatory criterion for rheumatoid arthritis; palm margarine used in white coffee roasting should be confirmed free of trans fat, and consumption should be limited to 1–2 servings daily to avoid caffeine-related sleep disruption that may worsen RA symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 2-in-1 White Coffee (No Sugar) is an instant beverage product popular in Singapore and Malaysia, consisting of instant coffee granules premixed with a non-dairy creamer but without any added sugar. It is prepared by dissolving one sachet in hot water, producing a smooth, milky coffee drink with a mild, slightly sweet flavour derived solely from the creamer. The 'white coffee' style originates from Ipoh, Malaysia, where coffee beans are traditionally roasted with palm margarine rather than sugar, yielding a lighter roast colour and a less bitter, more mellow taste compared to conventional dark-roasted kopi. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 56 kcal, 0.61 g protein, 2.34 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from the palm-based creamer), 7.98 g carbohydrate, 1.43 g sugar, 0.53 g dietary fibre, and 27 mg sodium. The low sugar content and absence of added sugar make it suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension, provided the creamer does not contain glucose syrup or maltodextrin. The estimated glycemic index is low (~41), reflecting the minimal sugar and the fat-mediated slowing of gastric emptying. Clinically, the main consideration is the saturated fat contribution from the palm-based non-dairy creamer, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Coffee polyphenols (chlorogenic acids) are present and may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit. This beverage is widely consumed at breakfast and as a morning pick-me-up across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian diaspora.
common_user_queries: Is 2-in-1 white coffee suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in 2-in-1 white coffee no sugar?
Is instant white coffee without sugar good for hypertension?

Is kopi putih tanpa gula healthy?
What is the GI of instant white coffee?
Can I drink white coffee if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010005, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is estimated from typical palm-based non-dairy creamer fatty acid composition and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly flagged as such.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.52 g/100 g) is estimated at approximately 65% of total fat based on the typical fatty acid profile of palm-based non-dairy creamer, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This estimate is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar non-dairy creamer products. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific instant white coffee premix are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values as reported by the approved source. 4. GI value of 41 is an analogous estimate based on instant coffee with non-dairy creamer entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. 5. The 'white coffee' roasting style traditionally uses palm margarine; consumers and clinicians should verify the product label for trans fat content. 6. This record covers the prepared liquid beverage as consumed (per 100 g of prepared drink).
